blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following formula:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This formula expresses the total differential of a function in terms of its partial derivatives.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$.

blocks.description: The figure illustrates the concept of change in position on a surface given a change in variables. It shows a point $P$ moving to a new position $Q$, highlighting the relationship between changes in $x$, $y$, and their effect on $z$.

blocks.latex: \Delta z = \Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} + \Delta y \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}
blocks.description: This equation shows how changes in $x$ and $y$ influence the change in $z$.

blocks.text: Let $P$ be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let $P$ move to a new position $Q$ near to $P$. The change of position is most easily referenced to a diagram (Fig. 21).

The change in $z$ due to the change in $x$ is the product of the change in $x$ multiplied by the rate at which $z$ is changing with respect to $x$; and this fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram.
